nativeRange.habitat: Coral reefs at depths of approximately 1–25 meters; typically observed in pairs

decorPreferences: ample live rock for hiding and grazing

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented feeding mainly on coral polyps along with a variety of benthic invertebrates. In captivity, a varied diet is generally advised — dried seaweed and Spirulina-based foods alongside vitamin-enriched mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, and finely chopped meaty marine foods. Multiple small feedings per day tend to improve long-term acclimation.

goodWith: mated conspecific (when introduced as an established pair)
avoidWith: additional conspecifics outside an established pair

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso; 4554x3036. Tack-sharp full-body profile clearly showing the pale face with dark eye band, fine vertical dark bars on a grey body, and yellow caudal-peduncle marking. Best diagnostic and quality match.
Diego Delso; 4657x3105. Sharp full-body shot against a natural reef backdrop, same diagnostic features well shown; strong second angle.
Rui Ribeiro; 1024x768. In-focus lone individual in bright blue open water, whole fish framed; different setting for variety. Slight blue cast.

summary: The White Faced Butterflyfish (Chaetodon mesoleucos) is a Red Sea species with a pale-grey body, vertical black bars, and a distinctive white face marked by a dark eye band. Typically observed in pairs on coral reefs, it is uncommon in the aquarium trade but generally reported to acclimate well in a mature, peaceful system.
careNotes:
The White Faced Butterflyfish is native to the Red Sea, Gulf of Aden, and waters around Socotra, where it has been documented inhabiting coral reefs from roughly 1 to 25 m depth, typically in monogamous pairs. Although uncommon in the aquarium trade, it has been reported as not especially difficult to maintain in a well-established system with peaceful tankmates.

This species is generally considered not reef-safe — it has been documented feeding on coral polyps and benthic invertebrates in the wild, and is best kept in a fish-only or fish-only-with-live-rock (FOWLR) system rather than a stony-coral reef. Conspecific aggression has been reported, so additional Chaetodon mesoleucos should only be added as a confirmed, simultaneously-introduced pair. Adequate live rock for shelter and grazing is generally advised, along with stable, well-aged water chemistry typical of mature reef systems.
breedingNotes: No established captive-breeding accounts for this species are known in the aquarium hobby.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source — adult size, care level (Expert Only), reef-compatibility (No), minimum tank size 125 gal, temperature 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, omnivore diet guidance, conspecific aggression note.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked Red Sea native range, omnivore diet, not reef-safe; disagrees with LiveAquaria on adult size (5 in vs 6 in), minimum tank size (75 gal vs 125 gal), and care level (Intermediate vs Expert).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Chaetodontidae / Acanthuriformes), maximum size (13 cm TL, common 12 cm), Western Indian Ocean distribution (Red Sea, Gulf of Aden), wild temperature range (26.9–29.3°C), depth range (5–20 m), and pair-bonding/oviparous reproduction.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy, distribution (central Red Sea, Gulf of Aden, Socotra), depth range (1–25 m), size (12–13 cm), reported diet (coral polyps and benthic invertebrates), pair association on the reef, and the note that the species is 'not difficult to keep in captivity'. Synonym Chaetodon hadjan recorded.

fish.breedingNotes: The White Faced Butterflyfish is an egg-scattering (oviparous) species that spawns in bonded pairs in the wild. No established captive-breeding accounts are known in the aquarium hobby.
